ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw galangal root as a food item; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of raw galangal is approximately 6 g (1 tablespoon of thin slices), used as an aromatic flavouring in soups, curries, and stir-fries.

nutrient_source.title: Galangal, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for galangal, raw (F14040047). All core macros and available micronutrients are sourced from this record.

gi_source.title: Ginger (Zingiber officinale) — analogous aromatic rhizome; GI estimated from peer-reviewed literature on low-GI aromatic spice rhizomes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / peer-reviewed analogy
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw galangal exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Galangal is a low-carbohydrate aromatic rhizome (7.79 g carbohydrate/100 g, ~2 g fibre) closely related to ginger. GI is estimated at approximately 28 (low) by analogy with ginger and other aromatic rhizomes documented in peer-reviewed GI literature. Glycemic load per 6 g serving = (28 × 5.79 net carbs / 100) × 0.06 ≈ 0.13, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium-to-sodium ratio (397 mg potassium vs 82 mg sodium per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Galangal's favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (approximately 4.8:1) supports DASH diet principles for blood pressure management; however, it is used in small culinary quantities so the absolute mineral contribution per serving is modest.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (7.79 g/100 g) with estimated low glycemic index (~28) and dietary fibre (~2 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Galangal has a negligible glycemic impact at typical culinary doses (approximately 6 g per serving); persons with Type 2 Diabetes can use it freely as a flavouring spice while monitoring the overall carbohydrate content of the dish.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.17 g/100 g), negligible saturated fat, and polyphenolic compounds (galangin, kaempferol) with documented antioxidant activity
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Galangal is a very low-fat, low-calorie aromatic spice; its polyphenolic content (galangin, kaempferol) has demonstrated antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties in vitro, and it can be incorporated freely into a heart-healthy diet as a flavouring ingredient.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Galangin and polyphenolic compounds with documented anti-inflammatory properties analogous to gingerols in ginger
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Galangal contains galangin and related polyphenols with anti-inflammatory activity; use in material culinary quantities may provide modest benefit, but individual sensitivity to aromatic spices should be monitored during active RA flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Galangal (Alpinia galanga), also known as greater galangal, blue ginger, or Siamese ginger, is a rhizomatous spice plant native to Southeast Asia and widely used across Thai, Indonesian, Malaysian, and Singaporean cuisines. The raw root has a pale yellow-white to cream interior with a firm, fibrous texture and a distinctive spicy, citrusy, and slightly piney flavour that is sharper and more resinous than common ginger. It is used primarily as an aromatic flavouring base in dishes such as Thai tom kha gai (coconut galangal soup), Indonesian rendang, Malaysian laksa, and Singaporean curry pastes. Nutritionally, raw galangal is very low in calories (35 kcal/100 g) and fat (0.17 g/100 g), with moderate carbohydrate content (7.79 g/100 g) and approximately 2 g dietary fibre per 100 g. It provides a notably high potassium content (397 mg/100 g) relative to its sodium content (82 mg/100 g), making it favourable for blood pressure management. Its polyphenolic compounds — particularly galangin and kaempferol — have demonstrated antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties in peer-reviewed research. The glycemic impact at typical culinary serving sizes (approximately 6 g) is negligible, making it suitable for persons managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. Galangal is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: Is galangal the same as ginger?
Is galangal good for blood pressure?
meal_context: Thai tom kha gai soup base

confidence_reason: Core macros and available micronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14040047), an approved primary source with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from ginger-family rhizome data; this is documented transparently. Sugar and dietary fibre values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for galangal (closest approved secondary source) and are clearly noted.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source; galangal is a very low-fat root with negligible saturated fat). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant food, naturally contains no cholesterol). 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and full micronutrient panel for raw galangal are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14040047) or the USDA FoodData Central galangal entry as of 2026-04-16. 3. sugar_g (1.5 g/100 g) and dietary_fibre_g (2.0 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for spices, galangal (closest approved secondary source) as HPB does not report these sub-fractions; values are plausible and consistent with the carbohydrate total. 4. GI of approximately 28 is an analogous estimate based on ginger and aromatic rhizome data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for raw galangal exists in any approved GI source as of 2026-04-16. 5. The input record contained a logical inconsistency in the rheumatoid_arthritis health_suitability entry (suitable: false but reason cited anti-inflammatory properties); this has been corrected to suitable: true with concern_level: low in health_context. 6. This is a generic raw ingredient record; no Singaporean-dish multilingual naming requirements apply (food is a Southeast Asian spice ingredient, not a Singaporean dish). Local-language alias 'Lengkuas' (Malay/Indonesian) is included in aliases.
